items.title: Marsh–Oberhuber classification
items.contentHtml:
Marsh-Oberhuber Classification

  • Marsh 0: normal mucosa.
  • Marsh 1: increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (>40/100 enterocytes) without atrophy.
  • Marsh 2: crypt hyperplasia + lymphocytosis intraepithelial.
  • Marsh 3 (subdivided into a, b, c): partial villous atrophy (3a), subtotal (3b) or total (3c).
  • Marsh 4: total villous hypoplasia (rare, associated with refractory lymphoma).
  • The classification is diagnostic in patients with positive serology and a gluten diet; Marsh 3 is the classic find.

items.title: Anti-transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA)
items.contentHtml:
Anti-transglutaminase IgA as screening

  • First line of screening: anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) antibodies.
  • Sensitivity >95% and specificity ~98% in active celiac disease.
  • Confirmation with anti-endomysium IgA (EMA) yes tTG-IgA is doubtful (specificity ~100%).
  • In symptomatic children with tTG-IgA >10 times the upper limit of normal and positive EMA, a biopsy can be skipped (ESPGHAN criteria).
  • False positives: cirrhosis, heart failure, psoriasis.

items.title: HLA DQ2 and DQ8
items.contentHtml:
Association with HLA DQ2/DQ8

  • 90-95% of patients express HLA-DQ2 (DQA1*05:01/DQB1*02:01); the rest express HLA-DQ8 (DQA1*03/DQB1*03:02).
  • The absence of both alleles has a negative predictive value of >99% to rule out celiac disease.
  • It is not diagnostic on its own: ~30% of the general population carries DQ2/DQ8 without developing the disease.
  • Useful in doubtful cases. (negative serology with clinical suspicion) and to rule out disease in first-degree relatives.

items.title: Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)
items.contentHtml:
Dermatitis herpetiformis

  • Pathognomonic cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease.
  • Puritic vesicles grouped on elbows, knees, buttocks and scalp.
  • Deposits of granular IgA in papillary dermis (direct immunofluorescence) = gold standard diagnosis.
  • It affects duodenum in >80% of patients, although the majority do not present digestive symptoms.
  • Responds to gluten-free diet; may require dapsone for rapid control of pruritus.

items.title: IgA deficiency and false negatives
items.contentHtml:
IgA deficiency

  • Selective IgA deficiency (1:500) causes false negatives in tTG-IgA and EMA-IgA.
  • Caution: measure total IgA simultaneously with screening.
  • If total IgA is low (<0.07 g/L in adults), request tTG-IgG or DGP-IgG (deaminated gliadin peptides IgG) as an alternative.
  • IgA deficiency is more common in celiac patients than in the general population.

items.title: gluten ataxia
items.contentHtml:
Gluten ataxia

  • Immune-mediated neurological disorder associated with celiac disease.
  • Clinical: cerebellar ataxia of insidious onset, dysarthria, nystagmus, without digestive symptoms in >50%.
  • Diagnosis: anti-transglutaminase 6 antibodies. (TG6)and/or anti-gliadin IgG; Cerebellar atrophy on MRI.
  • Partial response to gluten-free diet; The damage can be irreversible if it is established late.

items.title: Classic malabsorption syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Classic malabsorption syndrome

  • Triad: chronic diarrhea (steatorrhea), weight loss and abdominal distention.
  • Studies: steatorrhea in 72-h stools (>7 g/day), low D-xylose (affects proximal mucosa), test altered Schilling (B12 malabsorption if it affects the ileum).
  • Anemia due to deficiency of iron, folate and vitamin B12 (in order of frequency).
  • Hypocalcemia, hypoalbuminemia, deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

items.title: Gluten-free diet (GFD)
items.contentHtml:
Gluten-free diet for life

  • Only effective treatment: strict gluten-free diet for life.
  • Exclude wheat, barley, rye (and derivatives); pure uncontaminated oats are tolerated by most.
  • Clinical improvement in weeks; serological normalization in 6-12 months; histological recovery in 1-2 years.
  • Do not start diet before completing biopsy (risk of false negatives).
  • Supplement: iron, calcium, vitamin D, folic acid until correction.

items.title: Intestinal lymphoma and complications
items.contentHtml:
Intestinal lymphoma late complication

  • Main neoplastic complication: enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL).
  • Relative risk increased 10-50 times in untreated or refractory celiac disease.
  • Presentation: abdominal pain, obstruction, perforation, mass in jejunum/ileum.
  • Others complications: refractory celiac disease (type I and II), ulcerative jejunitis, intestinal adenocarcinoma.
  • The gluten-free diet significantly reduces the risk.

items.title: Duodenal biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Key biopsy affects duodenum

  • The lesion is patchy and more severe in distal duodenum (duodenal bulb included).
  • At least 4-6 biopsies of the distal duodenum and 1-2 of the bulb are required to avoid false negatives.
  • Technique: upper endoscopy with biopsy; capsule endoscopy is an alternative in doubtful cases.
  • The biopsy is performed with the patient consuming gluten (minimum 10 g/day for 6 previous weeks).
  • Key finding: villous atrophy with crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytosis.

items.title: Refractory iron deficiency anemia
items.contentHtml:
Refractory iron deficiency anemia

  • Iron deficiency anemia is the most common hematological manifestation in celiac disease (up to 50% of adults).
  • Refractory to oral treatment with iron if gluten-free diet is not instituted.
  • Pathophysiology: malabsorption of iron in the duodenum due to atrophy villous.
  • DDx: occult gastrointestinal bleeding, autoimmune gastritis, B12/folate deficiency.
  • In unexplained iron deficiency anemia, screening with tTG-IgA is recommended.

items.title: Osteoporosis in celiac disease
items.contentHtml:
Osteoporosis due to calcium malabsorption

  • Malabsorption of calcium and vitamin D secondary to villous atrophy and steatorrhea.
  • Compensatory secondary hyperparathyroidism that accelerates bone loss.
  • Bone mineral density reduced in >50% of patients at diagnosis.
  • Indication: bone densitometry (DXA) in all adult celiac patients at diagnosis.
  • Treatment: gluten-free diet + calcium supplements (1000-1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-2000 IU/day).
